funFacts.1: Hydrogen is the most abundant element in the universe, making up about 75% of all normal matter.
funFacts.2: Helium is so light that Earth's gravity isn't strong enough to hold it; it escapes into space!
funFacts.3: Lithium is the lightest metal and is used in rechargeable batteries for phones and electric cars.
funFacts.4: Beryllium is incredibly strong but lightweight, making it perfect for spacecraft and satellites.
funFacts.5: Boron is used in fireworks to create vivid green colors.
funFacts.6: Carbon is the foundation of all life on Earth and can form diamonds or the graphite in your pencil.
funFacts.7: Nitrogen makes up 78% of the air we breathe and is used to keep food fresh in packaging.
funFacts.8: Oxygen is highly reactive and forms compounds with almost every other element on the periodic table.
funFacts.9: Fluorine is so reactive it can burn holes through glass, but in small amounts (fluoride), it protects your teeth.
funFacts.10: Neon glows a bright reddish-orange when electrified, which is why it's famous for neon signs.
funFacts.11: Sodium is a soft metal that explodes when it touches water, but it's essential for life in the form of table salt.
funFacts.12: Magnesium burns with an incredibly bright white light and is used in flares and sparklers.
funFacts.13: Aluminum was once more valuable than gold! The Washington Monument is capped with an aluminum pyramid.
funFacts.14: Silicon is the basis for all modern electronics and computer chips.
funFacts.15: Phosphorus comes in white and red forms; red phosphorus is used on the side of matchboxes to strike matches.
funFacts.16: Sulfur is famous for its distinct 'rotten egg' smell, often found near volcanoes and hot springs.
funFacts.17: Chlorine is used to keep swimming pools clean and was used as a chemical weapon in WWI.
funFacts.18: Argon is an inert gas used inside lightbulbs to stop the glowing filament from burning up.
funFacts.19: Potassium is found in bananas and is crucial for keeping your heart beating properly.
funFacts.20: Calcium is essential for building strong bones and teeth, and it's also the main component of chalk.
funFacts.22: Titanium is as strong as steel but much lighter, making it ideal for jet engines and artificial joints.
funFacts.26: Iron is the main ingredient in steel and is responsible for making your blood red.
funFacts.28: Nickel is used to make stainless steel and is naturally magnetic.
funFacts.29: Copper is an excellent conductor of electricity and turns green when exposed to air (like the Statue of Liberty).
funFacts.30: Zinc is used to 'galvanize' other metals to prevent them from rusting.
funFacts.47: Silver reflects light better than any other metal, which is why it's used to make high-quality mirrors.
funFacts.79: Gold is incredibly malleable; a single ounce can be stretched into a wire 5 miles long!
funFacts.80: Mercury is the only metal that is liquid at room temperature and is highly toxic.
funFacts.82: Lead is very dense and blocks radiation, which is why you wear a lead apron during X-rays.
funFacts.92: Uranium is radioactive and is used as fuel in nuclear power plants.
